posts.name: p. raghib
posts.cooked:
I know tickets have already gone on sale, I was surprised not to see a thread already.

All in all, about what I expected. When I visited burgundy in November a number of producers told me they weren’t going to be able to make it this year for various reasons. Many of my favorites Dujac, Lafarge, Clerget, Leflaive, Roulot, Roumier, Rousseau, Duroche, Faiveley, Clos des Lambrays, Genot-Boulanger, Trapet aren’t coming this year. It is good to see some new producers Lamy, Leroux, Bruno Clair, Moreau-Naudet, Vocoret make it but all in all I think this is about the skinniest lineup in the past 20yrs.

I will probably attend the verticals on Friday and the main tasting Saturday afternoon, but that’s about it. I did the gala in 2020 which was a ton of fun, but it is still just a bit too much chaos for me. I’d love to know how many people they seat for the $9,500 Liger-Belair dinner…but a man has got to know his limitations.

anyone else planning on making it?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: If I am super lucky, I am going to try!

posts.name: Ethan Abraham
posts.cooked: Perhaps the verticals, maybe the gala, assuming things are calm enough to do large group events again. Just as happy to do smaller dinners outside though. The LA Lafon dinner looks group, I wish the D’Angerville dinner had some back vintages!

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked:
Yeah, I’ll be in LA. Grand Tasting for now, waiting to see who’s joining the Gala dinner.

Paul, you said it was too much chaos. Care to elaborate?

posts.name: Alex Valdes
posts.cooked:
Perhaps the verticals, maybe the gala, assuming things are calm enough to do large group events again. Just as happy to do smaller dinners outside though. The LA Lafon dinner looks group, I wish the D’Angerville dinner had some back vintages!

Evan and I were discussing earlier. I am not signing up for any events as of now. I am a maybe on the gala dinner but I don’t know if it will be good or not. Have to see who is attending.

I am planning to put together some smaller dinners. If others are interested / are aware of dinners let me know.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: I’m doing some of the events. Gala will depend on whether or not enough of my friends go to put together a fun table. There will be some big fun dinners put together privately as well I’m sure.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
I’m leaning towards the Verticals. Grand tasting is mostly 2018 which I don’t have much interest in.

Gala dinner is unlikely though the thought that maybe I should do it just once is gaining on me a little.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
I’m struggling with whether to attend. Normally I love the Grand Tasting as a way to really get a handle on a vintage and discover a few new things. While I agree the list is thin on old stand-bys, the chance to taste a bunch of new and/or up and coming producers is attractive. But … very disappointing that this is mostly 2018s. Weird. I attended in 2020 and that was all 2017s. Is next year ‘19s? Or 20s? It’s bad enough when La Paulee is a year behind release so that much is already gone from the market. But two years?

If it weren’t so 2018 dominant, I’d much more likely attend. The d’Angerville seminar would be interesting, and the Off the Grid tasting has been excellent (if crowded) in the past. This year’s Verticals line up actually looks better than it has been the last few years.

But then there’s Covid. Spitting with several hundred people who just flew in from around the world … not sure I can get comfortable with that yet.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I signed up for the Verticals and the Hudelot-Nöellat lunch.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
But then there’s Covid. Spitting with several hundred people who just flew in from around the world … not sure I can get comfortable with that yet.

And that’s why I haven’t signed up for the Verticals yet.

I went to La Fete and spent a lot of the day thinking, “this is probably one of the stupider things I’ve done recently”.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: In for Verticals. That’s it.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Huge difference in the producer participation between NY and LA, bit price is the same.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Gala is more about the BYO than the wineries. And the chances are you will taste wines there that you will never have the chance to taste again.

It took me a couple of years to recover from that and the after parties, just in time for the next one.

posts.name: Fred_Daner
posts.cooked: For those who’ve been how crowded is the Vertical’s tasting usually compared to the Grand Tasting ?

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked: Just signed up for the Verticals so I’ll see some of you there

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I have found the Grand Tasting to be more crowded than the Verticals, but given the choices this year, it seems like more people are leaning towards the Verticals. The picture is from the Grand Tasting in 2018 and as you can see it is pretty crowded, similar to a La Fête Grand Tasting. The gentlemen in the foreground need no introduction. The Gala makes the Grand Tasting seem tame.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: I attended several times when it was held in San Francisco, and really enjoyed it. Unfortunately cannot make the trip to SoCal for this one. Curious why they moved it?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Just signed up for the Verticals so I’ll see some of you there

I was there the whole time but didn’t see you. Did you go? I bumped into Jayson Cohen and Corey Miller.

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked: Loved the pics by Jayson and Jon on IG. Thanks for the fun look inside the event(s)!

posts.name: Dan Almodovar
posts.cooked: I’ll be at the Volnay tasting with William Kelly later today, I skipped the Grand tasting due to the 2018 vintage and running a little thin on other producers I enjoy although discovering new one’s is always a treat. Looking forward to hearing about everyone’s experience.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Waiting on the Volnay tasting to start at Daniel. Beautiful day in NYC. Vertical tasting was pleasant but only a couple of heavy hitters. Ligier B-A was there along with Mugneret Gibourg. Nice to taste Ben Leroux wines, he is doing a great job

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I had a great lunch yesterday at Gabriel Kreuther with the wines of Hudelot-Noëllat and Matro. I ran into lots of folks at the verticals and sampled some tasty wines. General theme was '17 whites are great and '11 whites and reds were drinking very well. I overheard a couple of winemakers say the '11 are their favorites for current consumption. Not everyday that you get seated at lunch with William Kelley and Guillame d’Angerville and get to pour them a glass of '12 DP. Lot’s of great conversation.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
My one complaint: The hand sanitizer at the entrance was vicious and persistent. Even after leaving my hands under running water several times, I could still pick up some of the perfume.

Takeaways: A lot of '06s and '11s are drinking very nicely.

Note to self: Buy more Bize and Chandon de Briailles.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
There seemed to be a lot of 13’s being poured. Some hits some misses for that vintage. It is a quirky one for sure. I wonder if a lot of domains have a decent supply and would like to see them move? They tend to be relatively plentiful in the auction offerings at lower prices. The 12’s that were poured were quite good, perhaps the overlooked vintage of the teens.

FYI, things are definitely warmer this decade to the point where D’Angerville stated that he has not chaptalized in 10 years because the grapes are consistently ripe.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: I loved a lot of 13s at the Verticals. (I tasted every red wine but I inadvertently missed whites from Moreau Naudet and skipped Christian Moreau.)

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:

I hate vicious hand sanitizer too!

posts.cooked:
Not everyday that you get seated at lunch with William Kelley and Guillame d’Angerville and get to pour them a glass of '12 DP. Lot’s of great conversation.

That was very gracious of you, thank you!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’ll be at the Volnay tasting with William Kelly later today

Thanks for coming! Hope it lived up to expectations

posts.name: Dan Almodovar
posts.cooked:
I’ll be at the Volnay tasting with William Kelly later today

Thanks for coming! Hope it lived up to expectations

It was great William just wish it was a little longer. You and Guillaume provided great insight on Volnay. I have some questions that I thought about later. When do you think most Volnay’s approach their optimum drinking window and could you comment on the 2017 vintage specifically. Hope to meet at an event in the future your kind of like a wine rock star at these events. Cheers

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Not everyday that you get seated at lunch with William Kelley and Guillame d’Angerville and get to pour them a glass of '12 DP. Lot’s of great conversation.

That was very gracious of you, thank you!

My pleasure, always glad to share. I wish I could have attended your Volnay seminar afterwards, but I had to get back to Jersey.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It was great William just wish it was a little longer. You and Guillaume provided great insight on Volnay. I have some questions that I thought about later. When do you think most Volnay’s approach their optimum drinking window and could you comment on the 2017 vintage specifically. Hope to meet at an event in the future your kind of like a wine rock star at these events. Cheers

Thanks!

I would say it’s very hard to generalize, as this has more to do with how the wines are grown and made, and their vintage, than their zip code! As you saw last night, 2017s for example are already very approachable, with a charming, succulent, supple style that they all tend to share, but at the same time plenty of space for distinctions of site and producer to show through, too. In fifteen years, I think they’ll be just as delicious, without every shutting down in the interim. By contrast, the 2015 Clos des Ducs we had (inter alia) over dinner was rather closed. I tend to prefer to drink my Burgundy in general age 20+ than age 10, personally, and I think for good producers in recent vintages that won’t be a problem. I have Lafarge '76 Volnay village in my cellar that’s still full of fruit today!
